accepted_answer: Hi,  This covers how to get a Desktop Shortcut and get your Taskbar icon back:     Left click on far left Icon in the Taskbar > then in the "Start" Window click on down Arrow at bottom left to go to "Apps by Category" Window > locate the App > right click on it and select "Open file location" > in the next Window that presents itself you right click on App from the list > run Mouse Cursor over "Send to" > select "Desktop (create shortcut)".  And while you are at the "Apps by Category" Window with it > right click on the particular App > select "Pin to Taskbar"   Cheers.

title: Edit Comment in Excel 2007
original_question: When editing comments in Microsoft Excel 2007 by right clicking and choosing “Edit Comments” that is usually the only task required. SYSTEM & SOFTWARE INFO Microsoft Excel 2007 SYMPTOMS & ERROR DETAILS When I right-click on an older, existing comment and select "Edit Comments", the program is completely unresponsive. This issue started happening within the last week. WHAT I HAVE ALREADY TRIED I tested adding new comments by clicking "Insert Comment". ADDITIONAL CONTEXT I discovered a workaround: if I right-click and choose "Show/Hide Comment" first, and then go back and choose "Edit Comments", it allows me to edit the text. ---

[Clarifying questions the support agent previously asked]
Hi,   This sounds odd. The usual culprit is a protected worksheet but in this case the menu option to edit comments doesn't appear so it suggests it's not that.   Can you enter new comments? Can you edit these comments in the normal way of right click and edit comment?
accepted_answer: Hi,   I have heard of this problem before but never seen it.   As a sensible precaution make a backup of you workbook.   Ensure the problem worksheet is the active (visible) sheet and try this VB code.   ALT+F11 to open vb editor. Right click 'ThisWorkbook' and insert module and paste the code below in on the right.   While still in VB editor, tap the F5 key to run the code, it should run very quickly so don't be surprised if nothing seems to have happened. Go back to the worksheet and test your comments.     Sub RepairComments() Dim CmntRange As Range Dim C As Range Dim Cmnt As String On Error Resume Next     Set CmntRange = ActiveSheet.Cells.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeComments)     If CmntRange Is Nothing Then         MsgBox "no comments on sheet"         Exit Sub     End If  For Each C In CmntRange     Cmnt = C.Comment.Text     C.Comment.Delete     C.AddComment     C.Comment.Text Text:=Cmnt Next End Sub

title: VLOOKUP for time stamp sometimes returns incorrect value?

[Clarifying questions the support agent previously asked]
Hi Hayden,   Have you tried the above steps? Please let us know if you need further help.   Thanks, ZixuanDominic
accepted_answer: Hi Hayden,   Have you viewed joeu2004Brittany's reply? I would like to explain that when we use VLOOKUP, by default, the table must be sorted in an ascending order. @ joeu2004Brittany, @AshishTroy Mathur, thanks for your suggestions.   @nimrodyanaiHayden, in your case, I would like to suggest you use Advance Filter. We don't need to sort the data and delete duplicate data manually.   Here are the detailed steps: 1.Create two sheets, named Original and Deduplicated. The original data is copied from the table you provided.  2.Navigate to Deduplicated, click Data>Advanced. The Advanced Filter dialogue box will appear. Select Copy to another location.    3.Click List range>Original, choose the list range. Click Copy to, choose where you want to place the filtered data. Select Unique records only.    4.Click OK, the filtered data will appear.    Regards, ZixuanDominic

title: Changing font styles used in the Navigation Pane or comment Balloons in Word 2016
original_question: I recently contacted the help desk for Office 2016 for the Mac about changing the font sizes in the Navigation Pane and in the Comment balloons. I could not figure out a way to do this from within the Navigation Pane or the Review menu.  I tried several levels of Microsoft Support but we couldn’t make it work. After hanging up, I kept trying for a while and here’s is what finally worked:  Navigation Pane:  Have the Navigation Pane open. Select Format/Styles from the Word menu. Choose “Document Map” style under the Styles pull-down menu. Choose the styles/fonts/sizes that you want. Click Apply.  Comment Balloons:  Have comment balloons displayed and then highlight some text from any comment. Select Format/Styles from the Word menu. Choose the ‘Comment Text’ style. Make your changes and then click Apply. This is new to me but it seems to work every time. I would appreciate seeing if others can validate this approach.

[Clarifying questions the support agent previously asked]
What cells did you apply the conditional formatting to? If you didn't apply the formatting to the area you want to have highlighted, it won't highlight that area. Thanks, Aaron
accepted_answer: I think Aaron means, in the cells in the column with numbers, add three conditional formatting rules to each cell. You can create the rules in one cell and then copy...paste special...formats to the other cells in the column.   One rule checks if the cell's value is equal to the cell that contains the MIN of the column and applies the color; the second rule checks if the cell's value is equal to the cell that contains the MAX of the column and applies that color; the third does the same for the average.  So only two to three cells would be colored in the column of numbers.

title: How can I by formula extract numbers from column without the Zero's?
original_question: example: What I have                         What I need        A                                       B 1     50                                     50 2     60                                     60 3     0                                       40 4     40                                     30 5     0 6     0 7     30 Thanks
simulator_context: CORE PROBLEM I need an Excel formula to extract numbers from one column into another, completely ignoring any cells that contain a zero and shifting the remaining non-zero numbers up so there are no gaps. SYSTEM & SOFTWARE INFO Microsoft Excel SYMPTOMS & ERROR DETAILS I have a list of numbers in Column A that includes zeros (for example: 50, 60, 0, 40, 0, 0, 30). I want Column B to output only the non-zero numbers in order (which would be 50, 60, 40, 30). ADDITIONAL CONTEXT To explain the exact logic I am looking for: if A1 = 50, then B1 should be 50. If A2 = 60, B2 should be 60. If A3 = 0, B3 should skip the zero and instead take the next non-zero number from A4 (which is 40), making B3 = 40. I need this to continue down the column so that Column B contains only numbers without any zeros.
raw_simulator_context:
[Further details provided by the user]
Hi,  the output is the column B    which is what I need, as example A1= 60 it goes to B1= 60, and also for A2= 50 goes to B2 = 50, but in A3= 0   I didn't want the zero amount to goes to B3 ignores the zero and take instead of it the next number if is not zero which is in A4 = 40 to be gone to B3 = 40 not zero, and so on , I need to ignores all the zeros to have column with numbers only without zeros.  Thanks

---

[Clarifying questions the support agent previously asked]
What is the output you are expecting against each rows?

Can you please explain a bit further . . If for example: A1 = 50, B1 - 50, do you want it to return 100, 5050 . . . or if A1 = 0, B1 = 60, do you want it to return 060, 60 or 6 . . .
accepted_answer: Use the below formula and drag down - =IFJohnRROR(INDEJohnX($A$1:$A$1000,AGGREJohnGATEJohn(15,6,(ROW($A$1:$A$1000)-ROW($A$1)+1)/($A$1:$A$1000<>0),ROW(1:1))),"")

[Clarifying questions the support agent previously asked]
I have a similar issue; I didn't delete the whole temp folder but did delete everything inside it, and now the start menu will not open. Is there some way to remedy this without having to do a full system recovery? I can still more or less use the system without needing the start menu but it is kind of a pain. Is there some critical folder under the temp folder that pertains to the start menu that I could recreate or download from somewhere? Thanks.
accepted_answer: Some files can be deleted from the Temp folder, but not the folder itself because Windows uses it to hold temporary files in.  It's a bit like your car and it's gas tank. You can drain it, but you still have a fully functional automobile. But if you remove the gas tank, then you're not going to be going anywhere until you replace it.  You now find yourself in that situation with your computer. Until you replace the Temp folder and its contents, your machine is not going to function properly.  I think you're looking at having to Refresh the system in order to get it going again. What you'll have to do first is to create a Recovery disk and copy the manufacturer's partition to that. This means you'll have to go buy an external hard drive with at least 60GB free. But bearing in mind that the cost of a 60GB hard disk and one with a 500GB capacity is barely a matter of cents, you'll be better off with the large capacity drive which you can use for other purposes later.  To create the Recovery drive, hit Windows key to open the Start screen with the tiles and start typing the word "Recovery". You'll see the option called "Create a recovery drive" appear on the right. Click that and then click Next. Here's a screenshot.      Then follow instructions in this article: http://windows.microsoft.com/en-gb/windows-8/restore-refresh-reset-pc

Hello,  it's not quite clear what you want to resize. The Excel window? Where does PDF come in? What do you mean with "program image"? Can you post a screenshot and point to the element you want to resize?
accepted_answer: You can only resize a window that is not maximized.  In the top right corner of every window, regardless of what application it is, you see icons to minimize, maximize/restore and the X for exit. When a window is maximized, it cannot be resized. You need to click the maximize/restore icon to restore the window. It is a toggle switch. The first screenshot shows what it looks like in a maximized window that takes up the whole screen. The second screenshot shows what it looks like if the window is not maximized. This window's borders can be dragged.     Another quick way in Windows 7 and higher to see windows side by side is to select one window, hold down the Window key on your keyboard and hit the right arrow. That will send the window to the right hand side of the screen and it will take up half of the monitor. In Windows 10 you now can click any of the other open windows and it will be arranged on the left hand side of the screen. In older Windows versions you need to repeat the step to select a window and use the Windows key with the left arrow.

Are any of the captions in text boxes?
accepted_answer:
If a graphic is wrapped, Word automatically creates the caption in a text box; if the graphic is In Line With Text, the caption is ordinary paragraph text. If the graphics in question are not wrapped, then obviously the text boxes are unneeded. In older versions of Word, there was an option to convert the text box to a frame, and you could then select Remove Frame in the Format Frame dialog. In Word 2010, I think you'll just have to cut the text from the text box, paste it into a new paragraph (in the Caption style), and then delete the empty text box. It looks like you've got just five to do, so perhaps that won't be too onerous.

The following macro embeds all Shapes & InlineShapes in a document in tables as InlineShapes with a row for Captions. Whatever positioning applied to the original Shape object will apply to the table also. As coded, the ‘MakeImageTable’ macro limits the inserted InlineShape dimesnsions to 7.5*5.0cm. That code can, of course, be edited/omitted, in which case, so too can the corresponding code that re-sizes the inserted InlineShape to fit.  To have the caption row appear above the image, instead of below, change the Rows(1) references to Rows(2), change the Cell(1, 1) references to Cell(2, 1) and change the change the Cell(2, 1) references to Cell(1, 1).  Note that, due to the extra space taken up by the caption row, all except for the first of the document's pictures are liable to shift position slightly.  Sub AddImageCaptionTables() Dim iShp As InlineShape, Rng As Range, Tbl As Table Dim i As Long, PicWdth As Single, PicHght As Single, VPos As Single Dim HPos As Single, VRel As Long, HRel As Long, BShp As Boolean With ActiveDocument   For i = 1 To .InlineShapes.Count     If .InlineShapes(i).Range.Information(wdWithInTable) = False Then       With .InlineShapes(i)         .LockAspectRatio = True         PicWdth = .Width         PicHght = .Height         Set Rng = .Range       End With       With Rng         If .Characters.Last.Next.Text = vbCr Then .Characters.Last.Next.Text = vbNullString         .InlineShapes(1).Range.Cut       End With       BShp = False: VRel = 0: HRel = 0: VPos = 0: HPos = 0       Call MakeImageTable(Rng, PicWdth, PicHght, BShp, VRel, HRel, VPos, HPos)     End If   Next   While .Shapes.Count > 0     BShp = True     With .Shapes(1)       .LockAspectRatio = True       PicWdth = .Width       PicHght = .Height       VRel = .RelativeVerticalPosition       HRel = .RelativeHorizontalPosition       VPos = .Top       HPos = .Left       Set iShp = .ConvertToInlineShape     End With     With iShp       Set Rng = .Range       .Range.Cut     End With     Call MakeImageTable(Rng, PicWdth, PicHght, BShp, VRel, HRel, VPos, HPos)   Wend End With End Sub  Sub MakeImageTable(Rng As Range, PicWdth As Single, PicHght As Single, BShp As Boolean, _   VRel As Long, HRel As Long, VPos As Single, HPos As Single) Dim Tbl As Table, sngScl As Single, iShp As InlineShape 'Constrain maximum image size If PicWdth > CentimetersToPoints(7.5) Then   sngScl = PicWdth / CentimetersToPoints(7.5)   PicWdth = PicWdth / sngScl   PicHght = PicHght / sngScl End If If PicHght > CentimetersToPoints(5) Then   sngScl = PicHght / CentimetersToPoints(5)   PicWdth = PicWdth / sngScl   PicHght = PicHght / sngScl End If 'Create & format the table Set Tbl = Rng.Tables.Add(Range:=Rng, Numrows:=2, NumColumns:=1) With Tbl   .Borders.Enable = True   .Columns.Width = PicWdth   .TopPadding = 0   .BottomPadding = 0   .LeftPadding = 0   .RightPadding = 0   .Spacing = 0   .Rows(1).HeightRule = wdRowHeightExactly   .Rows(1).Height = PicHght   With .Rows     .LeftIndent = 0     If BShp = True Then       .WrapAroundText = True       .HorizontalPosition = HPos       .RelativeHorizontalPosition = HRel       .VerticalPosition = VPos       .RelativeVerticalPosition = VRel       .AllowOverlap = False     End If   End With   With .Cell(1, 1).Range     With .ParagraphFormat       .SpaceBefore = 0       .SpaceAfter = 0       .LeftIndent = 0       .RightIndent = 0       .FirstLineIndent = 0       .KeepWithNext = True     End With     .Paste     ' Resize the inserted InlineShape     Set iShp = .InlineShapes(1)     With iShp       .Width = PicWdth       .Height = PicHght     End With   End With   With .Cell(2, 1).Range     .Style = "Caption"     .End = .End - 1     .InsertAfter vbCr     .InsertCaption Label:="Figure", TitleAutoText:=" ", Title:="", _       Position:=wdCaptionPositionBelow, ExcludeLabel:=0     .Paragraphs.First.Range.Characters.Last.Text = vbNullString     .Borders(wdBorderLeft).LineStyle = wdLineStyleNone     .Borders(wdBorderRight).LineStyle = wdLineStyleNone     .Borders(wdBorderTop).LineStyle = wdLineStyleNone   End With End With End Sub  To work with just the selected image (or multiple images in a selected range), change: With ActiveDocument to: With Selection and change:   While .Shapes.Count > 0     With .Shapes(1) to:   While .ShapeRange.Count > 0     With .ShapeRange(1)
